subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Quasi-Experimental Design and Reinforcement
stimulus: Michael is the regional manager for a car company and needs to give a presentation to the president of the company about how different management styles might relate to the total number of sales of the company’s new electric car. He hires an industrial/organizational psychologist to study three stores, each in a different city. Each store’s manager has their own distinct management style: In Store A, the manager has a supportive style (open and warm). In Store B, the manager has a strict style (rigid and severe). In Store C, the manager, who is Michael’s good friend, has a distant style (permissive and

parts.prompt: Explain why this study is not a true experiment.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that this study is not an experiment because the researcher did NOT  use random assignment. 

OR 

Response must indicate that the study did not include the manipulation of a variable.

required_elements: The study is not a true experiment because the psychologist did not randomly assign participants to his groups. The study used stores that already existed instead of manipulating an independent variable. The independent variable was not manipulated by the researcher in this study.
alternatives.correct_example: The study is not a true experiment because the psychologist did not randomly assign participants to his groups. The study used stores that already existed instead of manipulating an independent variable. The independent variable was not manipulated by the researcher in this study.

wrong_examples: Michael didn’t use random selection, so his study is not an experiment.

parts.prompt: Identify the implied dependent variable for the study if the study had been a true experiment.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the number of cars sold would be the dependent variable.

required_elements: The DV would be the number of cars bought by customers. The DV is cars sold.
alternatives.correct_example: The DV would be the number of cars bought by customers. The DV is cars sold.

parts.prompt: Calculate the mean of Store B’s sales.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the mean for Store B is 5.

required_elements: Response must indicate that the mean for Store B is 5.

parts.prompt: Explain why the location of the store is a confounding variable in this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the location of the store is affecting the dependent variable in  some specified way.

required_elements: Each management style was also in a different city, and people in each city may have different tastes in items and will purchase things differently. Cities with more people would most likely sell more units. Store A is in a higher-income area, so more cars are sold there.
alternatives.correct_example: Each management style was also in a different city, and people in each city may have different tastes in items and will purchase things differently. Cities with more people would most likely sell more units. Store A is in a higher-income area, so more cars are sold there.

parts.prompt: Explain how the halo effect could influence Michael’s interpretation of the findings.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Michael’s previous positive perception of Store C’s manager  will cause him to think more positively toward Store C’s sales data than he otherwise  would.

required_elements: Michael’s good feeling about the manager at Store C will make him think the data for that store are wrong.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael’s good feeling about the manager at Store C will make him think the data for that store are wrong.

required_elements: Michael is good friends with the manager of Store C, so that causes him to make a positive evaluation of the store’s performance.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael is good friends with the manager of Store C, so that causes him to make a positive evaluation of the store’s performance.

wrong_examples: Michael views the manager positively, but the data lead him to think he may have

parts.prompt: Explain why the hiring of an industrial/organizational psychologist was appropriate for this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that I/O psychologists are interested in optimizing or  improving performance OR solving problems in the workplace.

required_elements: This study is about which management style leads to the most sales, so an I/O psychologist would be best to help solve this problem.
alternatives.correct_example: This study is about which management style leads to the most sales, so an I/O psychologist would be best to help solve this problem.

required_elements: Michael wants to identify ways to improve managers’ performance, and an I/O psychologist can help him with this goal.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael wants to identify ways to improve managers’ performance, and an I/O psychologist can help him with this goal.

parts.prompt: Explain how Michael could use positive reinforcement to help him finish his presentation by the deadline.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate Michael could reward himself with something that he likes each  time he works on his presentation to make it more likely that he will keep working.

required_elements: Michael treats himself to a small piece of candy after he works for 20 minutes to encourage himself to keep working.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael treats himself to a small piece of candy after he works for 20 minutes to encourage himself to keep working.

wrong_examples: Michael is positive with himself about finishing the report on time.
Michael treats himself to candy after working on his presentation.
Michael eats a piece of candy and then begins to work on his presentation.
